one_line_takeaway: A small, replication-focused empirical study showing that FGSM adversarial examples crafted on a surrogate DNN transfer to a differently-built DDoS detector on CICDDoS-2019, dropping its accuracy from ~97% to ~61%, though the black-box effect is about half as strong as white-box and the setup assumes shared training data.
key_finding: The black-box transfer attack works but is markedly weaker than the white-box attack. As epsilon rises 0.0001->0.0009, the surrogate under white-box FGSM collapses from 99.05% accuracy (clean) to 23.85% (F1 99.01% -> 23.00%), while the target under transfer degrades from 97.02% to 61.15% accuracy (F1 97.01% -> 59.94%). So transferability is confirmed (a model the attacker never sees loses ~36 accuracy points from perturbations crafted on a differently-architected surrogate), but the effect is roughly half the magnitude of the white-box case, supporting the conclusion that white-box attacks are more severe while black-box transfer is the more realistic threat.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is an empirical demonstration that the black-box adversarial-transferability phenomenon holds in a deep-learning DDoS/network-attack detection system. The authors train two DNNs on the same CICDDoS-2019 subset but with deliberately different architectures and hyperparameters: a 'surrogate' the attacker fully controls and a 'target' the attacker treats as a black box (no access to gradients, weights, or architecture, but assumed access to the training data). FGSM adversarial perturbations are generated on the surrogate (a white-box attack on that model) and then transferred to the target at inference time. The claimed novelty is that, to the authors' knowledge, no prior work had empirically tested this untargeted black-box transferability specifically for a network-security / NIDS detector on the recent CICDDoS-2019 dataset. The many NIDS+adversarial studies described in Section 3/Table 1 (Clements, Usama, Pawlicki, Guo, Alhajjar, Sethi, Zhang, Han, Maarouf, Sarikaya, Debicha, etc.) are cited related work, NOT this paper's results, and are not numerically compared against. The paper also contributes a taxonomy of adversarial ML and an intentionally simple, replication-oriented methodology writeup (algorithms, hyperparameter tables) aimed at newcomers.
threat_model: Untargeted, black-box, evasion (inference-time) attack on a DL-based network intrusion / DDoS detection system. Adversary capabilities: no knowledge of the target model's architecture, gradients, weights, or hyperparameters, and can only query it (framed as API access), BUT is assumed to have access to the same training data. The adversary trains a surrogate DNN, uses white-box FGSM on it to craft small perturbations (epsilon 0.0001-0.0009), and relies on transferability so those perturbations also fool the target. Goal: reduce the detector's confidence/accuracy (cause DDoS traffic to evade detection), not force a specific target label. A key unaddressed constraint the paper itself acknowledges (Section 3) is that network-traffic features are structured/correlated/constrained, unlike image pixels, so FGSM perturbing all features may not yield realizable traffic; the paper perturbs feature vectors directly rather than validating packet-level realizability.
limitations: Author-stated: only FGSM is used (no JSMA/PGD/C&W/poisoning/extraction); no defense is implemented or evaluated; work is testbed-only (no real-time deployment); single dataset and simple architectures. Reviewer-added, grounded in the paper's own setup: (1) the perturbation epsilons (1e-4 to 9e-4) are extremely small on standardized features and no L-infinity budget interpretation or perturbation-size sanity check is given; (2) both models share the identical training data, so 'black-box' still assumes strong data access and the transfer result may be inflated versus a truly data-disjoint attacker; (3) no external baseline or comparison to prior transfer/black-box methods (Table 1 works are described, not benchmarked); (4) perturbations are applied directly in feature space with no validation that they correspond to realizable network traffic, which the paper itself flags as the hard part of the constrained domain; (5) a genuine labeling garble in Section 7.3: the heading says 'Black-box attack on the surrogate model' while the body opens with 'white box adversarial attack on the target model,' both mislabeling what Table 13 actually reports (black-box transfer to the target).

datasets_used: CICDDoS-2019 (Canadian Institute for Cybersecurity DDoS dataset); random subset of 227,148 samples (107,764 BENIGN, 119,384 DDoS); 60:40 split -> 136,288 train / 90,860 test; binary BENIGN vs DDoS classification
quantitative_results: Clean surrogate model: accuracy 99.05%, precision 98.98%, recall 99.01%, F1 99.01%
Clean target model: accuracy 97.57%, precision 97.61%, recall 97.57%, F1 97.57%
White-box FGSM on surrogate: accuracy 86.14% (eps=0.0001) down to 23.85% (eps=0.0009); F1 86.00% -> 23.00%
Black-box transfer to target: accuracy 97.02% (eps=0.0001) down to 61.15% (eps=0.0009); F1 97.01% -> 59.94%; precision 97.12% -> 64.39%; recall 96.94% -> 62.15%
Surrogate confusion matrix (clean): FP 745, FN 155; Target confusion matrix (clean): FP 1723, FN 482
Model sizes: surrogate 3,322 trainable params (3 hidden layers 60/50/30, 50 epochs); target 1,982 trainable params (2 hidden layers 50/25, 60 epochs)
Attack epsilon range 0.0001-0.0009 (nine values); larger epsilon monotonically reduces accuracy
baselines_compared: No external method baselines. Internal comparisons only: clean (no-attack) performance vs under-attack performance, and white-box (surrogate) vs black-box transfer (target) attack magnitude. Prior NIDS+adversarial works in Table 1 are described qualitatively but not quantitatively benchmarked against.
